food_subgroup: Stir-fried thin rice vermicelli breakfast plate with egg, fish cake, and bean sprouts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for economy bee hoon Singapore. Closest parent concept: rice vermicelli stir-fried dish (FOODON:03301710 — rice noodle dish). No exact match available as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice vermicelli — closest parent FoodEx2 code used for Singapore economy bee hoon hawker breakfast (rice vermicelli + fried egg + fish cake + bean sprouts + soy sauce). Match type: closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Singapore economy bee hoon composite dish. A0VRM (rice vermicelli) used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Singapore economy bee hoon. Not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of economy bee hoon comprising approximately 120g cooked thin rice vermicelli, one fried egg (~50g), one slice of fish cake (~30g), bean sprouts (~30g), and a light soy sauce drizzle, totalling approximately 250g and providing around 363 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Economy Bee Hoon — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutritional Information Portal + USDA SR Legacy component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutritional Information Portal; USDA SR Legacy (secondary component verification)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Foodo for Singapore hawker economy bee hoon. Individual component macros (egg, fish cake, bean sprouts, soy sauce) cross-verified against USDA SR Legacy to confirm composite per-100g values. The dish is a composite; per-100g values represent the weighted average across the 250g plate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Noodles and Rice Vermicelli — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 is based on thin rice vermicelli / rice noodle GI range of 58–61 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI Values. The economy bee hoon dish uses thin rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source (~120g cooked, ~48g available carbohydrate). GL per 250g plate = GI × available carbohydrate (g) / 100 = 58 × 53g / 100 ≈ 30.7. Confidence: 0.9 (analogous estimate; no direct GI test of the composite dish found in approved sources).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 950mg per 250g plate (380mg/100g), primarily from light soy sauce drizzle (~500mg Na) and fish cake (~180mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit economy bee hoon to occasional consumption and request reduced soy sauce; a single plate provides approximately 40–48% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg, and pairing with kopi or other hawker beverages can push total breakfast sodium to 1,100–1,400mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high glycemic load (GL ~31 per plate) driven by thin rice vermicelli (GI ~58, ~48g available carbohydrate per plate)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat economy bee hoon as a moderate-GL meal; the protein from the fried egg and fish cake (~16g per plate) moderately blunts postprandial glucose response, but portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is advisable to manage blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~950mg/plate) and moderate cholesterol (~200mg/plate from fried egg) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat is low at 1g/100g (2.5g/plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, economy bee hoon is acceptable occasionally but sodium load is the dominant concern; requesting minimal soy sauce and substituting the fried egg with a steamed or poached egg would reduce both sodium and dietary cholesterol meaningfully.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1g/100g), moderate sodium (~950mg/plate), and micronutrient contributions from fried egg (Vitamin D ~1.25mcg/plate) and bean sprouts (Vitamin C ~5mg/plate)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Economy bee hoon is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the low saturated fat profile is anti-inflammatory, the fried egg provides Vitamin D relevant to bone and immune health, and bean sprouts contribute Vitamin C, though sodium should be monitored within the daily budget.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economy bee hoon is one of Singapore's most iconic and affordable hawker breakfast dishes, served at virtually every coffee shop and hawker centre from early morning. A standard plate (~250g) consists of approximately 120g of cooked thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon), one fried egg (~50g), one slice of fish cake (~30g), bean sprouts (~30g), and a light drizzle of soy sauce. The rice vermicelli is typically pre-soaked and briefly stir-fried or tossed in a wok with minimal oil, giving it a soft, slightly chewy texture with a delicate savoury flavour from the soy sauce. The fried egg adds richness and protein, the fish cake contributes a springy, mildly fishy note, and the bean sprouts provide a fresh crunch and volume. Nutritionally, a 250g plate provides approximately 363 kcal, 16g protein, 8g fat, and 55g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at ~950mg per plate, dominated by the soy sauce drizzle (~500mg) and fish cake (~180mg). The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base is approximately 58 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of ~31 per plate — classified as medium-high. The dish is dairy-free and can be halal-certified depending on the stall. It is one of Singapore's most calorically efficient hawker breakfasts, offering a complete macronutrient profile at low cost. The 'economy' designation reflects its affordability and no-frills preparation. Clinical note: sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management; GL is moderate-high and relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management.
common_user_queries: how many calories in bee hoon

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the authoritative Singapore government nutritional database for hawker foods, with component-level cross-verification against USDA SR Legacy. GI value of 58 is well-supported by the University of Sydney GI Database for thin rice vermicelli. The dish composition (120g bee hoon + 50g fried egg + 30g fish cake + 30g bean sprouts + soy sauce) is the canonical Singapore hawker standard. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singapore hawker dish is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy as a composite entry; individual component fatty acid data could be estimated but is not reported as a verified composite figure. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in HPB Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy for this dish type). All core macros (protein_g 6.5, fat_g 3.2, carbohydrate_g 22.0) are non-zero and well-supported. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on thin rice vermicelli GI range 58–61 from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI test of the composite dish was found in approved sources. GL per plate = 58 × 53g available carbohydrate / 100 = 30.7. Sodium of ~950mg per 250g plate is the dominant clinical concern. The dish is not vegetarian or vegan due to egg and fish cake components. Halal status depends on individual stall certification. FoodOn URI is null — no exact FoodOn concept exists for Singapore economy bee hoon; closest parent is rice vermicelli stir-fried dish.
